title: U.S. Interest in Seabed Mining in Areas Beyond National Jurisdiction: Brief Background and Recent Developments
summary: In 1980, Congress passed the Deep Seabed Hard Mineral Resources Act (DSHMRA; 30 U.S.C. §§1401 et seq.) as an interim measure to allow the United States to proceed with seabed mining activities in areas beyond national jurisdiction (ABNJ) until an international regime was in place (i.e., the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea [UNCLOS]). DSHMRA established a framework for authorizing U.S. citizens (e.g.,...

title: The Role of U.S. In Situ Ocean Observation Networks: A Case Study of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation
summary: A climate tipping point generally refers to a “critical threshold” in an Earth system that when crossed can cause the system to shift into a new state. Scientists contend that crossing a tipping point may result in a new long-term or permanent state from which the system may be unable to rebound, even if the trigger for crossing the threshold is reversed. One potential tipping point is the “collapse” (i.e.,...

title: Cybersecurity: Considerations on Regulatory Harmonization
summary: The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) is simultaneously pursuing three major cybersecurity regulatory initiatives—each issued by a different agency, each addressing a different critical infrastructure sector, and each with its own definitions, timelines, and reporting requirements for cybersecurity incidents. Some stakeholders have raised concerns about the potential burdens that the three separate, but...

title: What Is Temporary Protected Status?
summary: Temporary Protected Status (TPS) has long been used as a humanitarian solution for migrants who are unable to return home safely. President Trump’s efforts to scale back such programs have further fueled the debate over the immigration policy’s use and scope.

title: Myths, Fables, and Hard Truths About AI Governance
summary: CFR President Michael Froman assesses the U.S. government’s de facto ban on Anthropic’s most powerful AI model and what it reveals about the U.S. regulatory framework for AI.

title: Why Rules-Based Orders Fail
summary: For a brief period after the Cold War, Americans persuaded themselves that the liberal order had become self-sustaining. And yet, any rules-based system elaborate enough to govern and interpret its own operations will eventually confront questions that its rules cannot answer. Originally published at Project Syndicate

title: John F. Kennedy’s “Ich bin ein Berliner” Speech
summary: In just four words, JFK bonded with the people of West Berlin and gave rise to an urban legend.

title: The Iran Deal Comes at a Cost to Israel. The White House Hasn’t Acknowledged It.
summary: The U.S.-Iran deal sidelines Israel, a critical ally, on issues affecting its core security interests. That may be tactically defensible, but the way Washington has handled it is not.

title: Have Rising Living Standards Pushed Marriage out of Reach?
summary: Have rising material expectations made marriage feel less attainable for today’s young adults? Marriage rates have steadily been declining for several decades. Roughly one-third of Gen Z is expected to remain single by age 45, and to perhaps never marry at all. A common reason young adults give for not being married is a lack of financial resources. And a popular narrative these days says that declining wages among...

title: Children Deserve Education, Not Gender Ideology
summary: From coast to coast, states such as California and New Jersey face no shortage of serious challenges, from the soaring cost of living and rising crime to homelessness and drug abuse. title: Swiss Miss Out on Chance To Save Their Nation From Immigrant Invasion
summary: Snow caps, yes: people caps, no. In a national referendum this week, the people of Switzerland voted against a hard cap of 10 million on the population. The vote was called by the conservative Swiss People’s Party in an effort to control the country’s population growth, which is driven entirely by immigration. Switzerland is a unicorn among the world’s nations: a successful multilingual state. The country was...

title: Remembering Alan Greenspan
summary: The Taylor rule, named after Hoover Institution fellow John Taylor, recommends high interest rates in order to prevent possible inflation. During recessions, it recommends low rates to provide stimulus. The Taylor rule’s main virtues are that it is predictable, and that it can prevent impulsive Fed behavior. If the economy does X, then the Fed will automatically respond with Y.

title: Israeli Ambassador Blasts Lebanon Cease-Fire Talks As A ‘Train Wreck’
summary: Hoover Institution fellow Zohar Palti expressed skepticism about the arrangement — particularly because Israel was not involved in creating it. “It’s not serious,” Palti said of the deconfliction mechanism. “It’s like a wedding without the groom or the bride.”

title: The Inconvenient Truth Meets Inconvenient Facts: 7 Errors In Al Gore’s Documentary
summary: Hoover Institution fellow Bjorn Lomborg said in the Financial Post that an average of 500,000 people died every year due to climate disasters in the 1920s. Today, under 10,000 people die, a decline of 97 per cent.

title: A Democrat-On-Democrat Showdown Is Erupting Over Chicago’s Schools
summary: [Subscription Required] Hoover Institution fellow Michael Hartney said Chicago is following a national trend. Local school-board elections, once low-budget affairs, have increasingly become nationalized, with millions of dollars flowing in.

title: Free Speech Victory For Counselors
summary: Hoover Institution fellow Eugene Volokh submitted a brief supporting neither party in the Chiles v. Salazar case, although the content of what he said seemed to favor Chiles. He emphasized that “speech cannot lose its protection just because it is relabeled conduct and then banned".

title: America’s “direct return” doctrine—and what it means for the world
summary: In Bosnia and Herzegovina, the United States is focused on doing business, at the expense of peace. There are ways for Europeans to acquire a veto over these destabilising activities; but the Trump administration may yet replicate this approach across the globe.

title: British politics ten years after Brexit
summary: Mark Leonard welcomes Marcus Roberts to discuss the legacy of Brexit and the latest developments in British politics

title: Trump's demands for loyalty are causing friction

title: She posted about ICE. Five months later, DHS agents told her to take her post down
summary: In January, Paigelynne Gonyea posted about the immigration surge in Minneapolis. This week, she was visited by ICE officials who claimed one of her posts had doxxed a federal agent.

title: John Bolton, former Trump national security adviser, pleads guilty in classified documents case
summary: Bolton pleaded guilty to one count of retaining national defense information while he was a national security adviser during President Trump's first term, saying: "And I am sorry for it."

title: Ex-NOAA employees re-create a valuable climate data site shut down by Trump
summary: Former NOAA staffers have launched a new website that provides climate information. It replaces a government site that was shut down when the Trump administration took office.

title: Trump's TPS policy is a 'job killer' and bad for Ohio, Gov. DeWine says
summary: A Supreme Court ruling this week cleared the way for the Trump administration to end temporary protected status for Haitians and Syrians. One of the states that could face the biggest impact is Ohio, where more than 10,000 Haitian migrants have settled. Republican Gov. Mike DeWine called the move a mistake. He joined Amna Nawaz to discuss the ruling and what it means for communities in his state.

title: Newsom urges a national 'billionaires' tax' while fighting one in California
summary: The governor announced his agenda a day after an influential health care union in California pledged to go forward with a ballot measure that would impose a one-time 5% tax on the assets of billionaires living in the state as of Jan. 1, 2026. Newsom opposes that measure.

summary: The administration has provided little evidence to back up some of his assertions about the Lincoln Memorial pool. Experts say other factors could have caused the major problems that have plagued it.

title: Supreme Court Clarifies Federal Arbitration Act Exemption for Transportation Workers
summary: Employment contracts often include a pre-dispute arbitration agreement in which the parties waive their right to litigate future disputes between them in court and instead agree to submit legal claims to binding arbitration before a neutral third party. The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) (9 U.S.C. §§ 1–16) generally requires federal and state courts to enforce arbitration agreements that fall under the Act’s broad...

title: Supply Chain, Energy, and AI Nexus
summary: The authors identify the inventory of critical electrical equipment for artificial intelligence (AI) data center operations and the impact of supply chain vulnerabilities on the U.S. power grid’s ability to meet AI demand by 2030.

title: Can LLM Agents Select and Engage with Biological Tools?
summary: Large language model agents are capable of performing initial interactions with biological tools (BTs), indicating that agents could lower expertise barriers for actors seeking to use BTs. Targeted testing of agent design capabilities is warranted.

title: AI agents put offensive cyber within reach of novices
summary: Agentic AI models now enable non-experts to conduct advanced offensive cyberattacks. This study finds AI agents can autonomously breach complex systems rapidly, highlighting urgent needs for evolved defence and better cyber risk assessments.

title: Forced Labor Contracts Threaten America’s Workplace Freedom
summary: Should the federal government step in and impose union contracts when labor negotiations stall? Rep. Donald Norcross, New Jersey Democrat, thinks so. His Faster Labor Contracts Act (H.R. 5408) passed the House of Representatives on June 9 by a 230-193 vote, with 20 Republicans joining the Democrats in support. Although the legislation aims to accelerate first contracts for newly unionized workplaces, it does so by...

title: From Washington, DC, to the States: Rethinking Head Start
summary: Social science research finds that Head Start, a federally funded preschool program for children from low-income families, does not prepare children for K–12 schools, nor does it improve their social skills. Reports on spending and safety also show that Head Start centers waste money and fail to protect children. After more than six decades and more than $240 billion in federal spending, Head Start is a poor option...

title: Inside Trump’s plans for retribution and other lesser-known allegations in ‘Regime Change’
summary: A new book from two Washington reporters reveals what took place inside the White House after President Donald Trump’s return to power.

title: Migrants not entitled to apply for asylum while standing on Mexico side of border, Supreme Court rules

title: Democratic socialists cemented power in New York. Next, the rest of the country?
summary: The Democratic Socialists of America won with support outside their usual base. Winning outside New York may be a different challenge.

title: Understanding How Impact Fees Shape Housing Development Feasibility and Local Fiscal Revenue
summary: High impact fees contribute meaningfully to housing production costs in California. A 25-percent reduction enables hundreds of new projects in high-cost cities; increased property and sales tax revenue offsets forgone fees in four to seven years.

title: Cultivating a Warrior Ethos in the Air Force
summary: RAND researchers examined how the U.S. Air Force can cultivate an Airminded Warrior identity, finding that the concept requires more development. They recommend several actions to strengthen this identity’s development in initial training and beyond.

title: Beyond Brexit—and back to Europe
summary: Those who follow only intergovernmental negotiations or debates in the media might think that nothing has changed in the decade since the United Kingdom's voters decided to leave the European Union. title: Brexit isn’t working: British voters are ready for a European future
summary: Ten years on from the referendum, the British electorate has moved on from its old divisions and—in a world of crisis—is embracing an ambitious move back towards the European Union
